title: [Coroutines] Implement elision for noop coroutines
author: NewSigma
description:
This patch proposes to implement noop coroutine elision. It contains two parts:

1. Lower `coro.noop` in CoroCleanup instead of CoroEarly, thereby exposing it to other middle end passes.
2. CoroCleanup erases resume and destroy calls for the noop coroutine. If a `coro.noop` ends up having no users, the pass erases the call to `coro.noop`, avoiding the allocation of its frame.

tests.test_body:
define void @fn() personality i32 0 {
  %frame = call noundef ptr @llvm.coro.noop()
  %resume = call ptr @llvm.coro.subfn.addr(ptr %frame, i8 0)
  call fastcc void %resume(ptr %frame)
  %destroy = call ptr @llvm.coro.subfn.addr(ptr %frame, i8 1)
  invoke fastcc void %destroy(ptr %frame)
          to label %done unwind label %unwind

done:                                             ; preds = %0
  ret void

unwind:                                           ; preds = %0
  %pad = landingpad { ptr, i32 }
          catch ptr null
  unreachable
}

; Function Attrs: nounwind memory(none)
declare ptr @llvm.coro.noop() #0

; Function Attrs: nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: read)
declare ptr @llvm.coro.subfn.addr(ptr nocapture readonly, i8) #1

attributes #0 = { nounwind memory(none) }
attributes #1 = { nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: read) }

comments.author: github-actions[bot]
comments.body:
<!--LLVM CODE FORMAT COMMENT: {clang-format}-->

:white_check_mark: With the latest revision this PR passed the C/C++ code formatter.

comments.author: NewSigma
comments.body:
Thanks for the code review. I agree with you that CoroElide is designed for coroutine allocation. I worked on CoroElide because

1. The approach requires that we see the `coro.noop` intrinsic, which may be exposed by other middle end optimizations.
2. The pattern is not restricted to presplitcoroutine. Consider:

``` C++
void fn() {
  std::noop_coroutine().resume();
}
```

Not sure if there is a realistic use case, though.

I tried to handle await_suspend specifically in CoroSplit, but optimizations do not work well for await_suspend wrappers(*), which is a blocking issue. In this revision, I erase `coro.noop` in CoroCleanup and removed unrelated changes, since CoroCleanup is late in pipeline and works for general functions.

(*) It seems clang forgot to add function attributes to await_suspend wrappers. The inliner refuses to inline callees into await_suspend because their attributes mismatch.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
>  It seems clang forgot to add function attributes to await_suspend wrappers. The inliner refuses to inline callees into await_suspend because their attributes mismatch.

Sounds like an important issue to me.

comments.author: tzcnt
tzcnt
comments.body:
Perhaps what I'm asking for could be generalized as "inlining indirect calls/jumps when the target is statically known". I noticed another opportunity to apply this optimization. In a simple coroutine

`static tmc::task<int> inc(int n) { co_return n + 1; }`

the compiler still emits an indirect call to the `inc(int).cleanup()` function (`callq  *0x8(%rbx)`) at the end, even though the body of `cleanup()` is just `ret`. My coroutines destroy themselves when they finish, so this call always happens statically at the end of `inc(int).resume()`.

So if we could consider the special member functions `resume()` and `cleanup()`, of any coroutine, for inlining rather than forcing them to be indirected, that would solve the problem.

comments.author: tzcnt
comments.body:
Upon further testing, `lowerCoroNoop` was not being called at all, since I was using libstdc++ which does not emit this intrinsic. I expect the majority of users are still working this way.

However, after switching to libc++ I'm not seeing any progress - the jump to `__NoopCoro_ResumeDestroy` is still happening. I see from your fix it expects the User to be `CoroSubFnInst` but in my code it's a PHINode:

```
_ZN3tmc6detail25awaitable_customizer_base19resume_continuationEv.exit.i.i: ; preds = %_ZN3tmc6detail12post_checkedEPNS_6ex_anyEONSt3__116coroutine_handleIvEEmm.exit.i.i, %land.lhs.true.i.i.i, %if.else32.i.i.i, %if.end20.i.i.i, %if.end.i.i.i, %if.then2.i.i.i
  %22 = phi ptr [ @NoopCoro.Frame.Const, %_ZN3tmc6detail12post_checkedEPNS_6ex_anyEONSt3__116coroutine_handleIvEEmm.exit.i.i ], [ %13, %if.else32.i.i.i ], [ %13, %land.lhs.true.i.i.i ], [ @NoopCoro.Frame.Const, %if.then2.i.i.i ], [ @NoopCoro.Frame.Const, %if.end.i.i.i ], [ @NoopCoro.Frame.Const, %if.end20.i.i.i ]

...

invoke.cont:                                      ; preds = %_ZN3tmc6detail25awaitable_customizer_base19resume_continuationEv.exit.i.i
  %27 = load ptr, ptr %22, align 8
  musttail call fastcc void %27(ptr nonnull %22)
  ret void
```

comments.author: NewSigma
comments.body:
> The most common use case for this is a simple await_suspend like this:

Thanks for the feedback. The pattern is slightly more complex. Noting that it resumes coroutine immediately after the phi node, we can hoist these resume calls and elide noop coroutine in `elideNoopCoro`.

> Perhaps what I'm asking for could be generalized as "inlining indirect calls/jumps when the target is statically known".

I think we have those optimizations in CoroElide, but they sometimes fail. Improvements are on the plan.

> I was using libstdc++ which does not emit this intrinsic. I expect the majority of users are still working this way.

I would expect libstdc++ support it. You can emit the intrinsic with __builtin_coro_noop() for now.

> in my code it's a PHINode:

It looks different. What is going on between the phi node and the resume call? There is nothing we can do if the logic is complex.

If there are no objections, I propose to leave the issue open and land this. A fixing will be submitted in another pr.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> If there are no objections, I propose to leave the issue open and land this. A fixing will be submitted in another pr.

Yeah, I think what @tzcnt  said should be in another issue. The PR itself is good to me.

comments.author: tzcnt
comments.body:
I don't have any objection to this PR. However it's linked as closing https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/135377 - which in my opinion it does not.

You said my code sample is a bit more complex. But I can't imagine a situation where it's useful to unconditionally return `std::noop_coroutine()`. This is something that would always be happening conditionally, inside a phi node. So if this PR does not work if the noop coro goes through a phi node, then while it may be technically correct, it won't help any real world use cases.

I'd be happy to be proven wrong. Can you show a snippet of useful C++ code where this will have a positive impact?

comments.author: bgra8
comments.body:
Heads-up @NewSigma  we (at google) have bisected something that looks like a miscompile to this revision.

I'm working on reducing the test case to a small reproducer.

comments.author: bgra8
comments.body:
@NewSigma I got a smaller reproducer here: [repro.cc.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24971069/repro.cc.zip)


Compilation command:
```
$ clang -O1 -std=gnu++20 -pthread -lstdc++ -o /tmp/repro -x c++ repro.cc
```

Building with the version before and then executing the binary will get exit code 0. Building at this revision and executing the binary will get exit code 1.

Can you please take a look?

comments.author: NewSigma
comments.body: Thanks for the feedback @bgra8 . This is similar to #91123. Given that resuming/destroying a no-op coroutine does nothing, I believe calling convention should not matter here. I’ll work on a patch shortly.
